title: Writing - Part 1: Youth Unemployment
description: Your class has listened to a podcast on youth unemployment and its consequences for young people entering the labour market today.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the challenges in your notes. You should explain which challenge you consider most significant, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Many graduates find their qualifications don't match what employers need.
Without experience, graduates can't get jobs; without jobs, no experience.
Young workers are always first to be fired in recessions.

structure.introduction: Introduce the problem of youth unemployment and state which two challenges you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first challenge (e.g. educational mismatch), using the opinions and your own ideas to develop the argument.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second challenge (e.g. lack of work experience), explaining why you consider it the most significant.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your points and reinforce your view on the most critical challenge.

content.modelAnswer:
Youth unemployment remains one of the most pressing socioeconomic issues of our time, with millions of young people worldwide struggling to secure meaningful work. Two factors that contribute significantly to this crisis are the mismatch between education and employment needs, and the persistent barrier of lacking work experience.

The disconnect between academic qualifications and labour market demands has grown considerably in recent decades. Universities continue to produce graduates in fields where vacancies are scarce, while sectors such as technology, engineering and healthcare face acute skills shortages. Young people invest substantial time and money pursuing degrees, only to discover their expertise is poorly suited to available positions. This misalignment not only wastes human potential but also generates disillusionment among an entire generation.

However, the most significant challenge is arguably the experience paradox — the expectation that entry-level candidates already possess professional experience. Employers increasingly demand prior work history even for junior roles, yet opportunities to gain such experience remain unevenly distributed. Students from disadvantaged backgrounds rarely have access to well-connected networks or unpaid internships, placing them at a considerable disadvantage compared to more privileged peers. This self-perpetuating cycle effectively locks many talented young people out of the labour market entirely.

In conclusion, while educational mismatch represents a structural failing in how societies prepare young people for work, the experience barrier is arguably the more immediate obstacle, as it prevents capable graduates from demonstrating their potential at all. Addressing this requires coordinated action from employers, educational institutions and governments alike.
